foundation.title: STRUCTURE
foundation.subtitle: Structure of a Program
foundation.content: A Java program is built entirely of 'Classes' and 'Methods'. EVERY piece of code must live inside a class. Execution always starts perfectly at the top of the sequence in the 'main' method.
foundation.scenario: A Formal Letter. You must start with the correct Header (Class), the correct Greeting (Main method), and you MUST end every sentence with a period (Semicolon) or it won't be sent.
foundation.learningOutcome: Code runs line by line in order

examples.title: Class Match
examples.explanation: If your file is App.java, your class MUST be 'public class App'.

examples.title: Main Method
examples.explanation: The specific portal the JVM enters to find and sequentially run your actual code.

examples.title: Statements
examples.explanation: Code lines end forcefully in semicolons forming strict sequences.

foundation.keyPoint: One File = One Primary Class. main() is the starting gate.

foundation.title: PRINT
foundation.subtitle: Print Statements
foundation.content: Java uses 'System.out.println()' to print data to the screen. It separates text and visual values cleanly, blasting information onto the output display.
foundation.scenario: A Shipping Department. 'System' is the warehouse, 'out' is the shipping dock, and 'println' is the command to load a box onto the truck and drive away.

examples.title: Printing Text
examples.explanation: Prints text and immediately moves the cursor to a brand new line.

examples.title: Printing Numbers
examples.explanation: Printing raw numeric values separately from double-quoted text.

examples.title: Multiple
examples.explanation: Chaining consecutive print commands logically.

foundation.keyPoint: System.out.println() for a new line. System.out.print() stays on the line.

foundation.title: FORMAT
foundation.subtitle: Output Formatting
foundation.content: Java allows you to format output using 'System.out.printf()'. You use spaces, line breaks (\n), and special '% ' separator markers to combine distinct variables perfectly inside a text sentence.
foundation.scenario: A Fill-in-the-Blank Letter. 'Dear %s, your balance is %d'. The %s and %d are empty slots where you insert the real Name and Math data right at the end.

examples.title: New Line Style
examples.explanation: Using \n inside quotes to slice the text output dramatically.

examples.title: Combining Values
examples.explanation: Using %s for Strings and %d for Decimal Integers cleanly separated.

examples.title: Float Formatting
examples.explanation: Using %.2f to aggressively force a decimal to only show two fixed spots.

foundation.title: VARIABLE
foundation.subtitle: Variables (What/Why)
foundation.content: Variables are Strictly-Typed named memory containers that hold raw data. You violently need them because data changes actively during a running system.
foundation.scenario: A Moving Box in a House. You label it heavily 'Kitchen Stuff', and put plates inside. To change values tomorrow, you could put cups inside. But it cannot hold 'Books'.

examples.title: Declaration
examples.explanation: Reserving RAM memory logically by claiming a distinct type and a name.

examples.title: Storing Data
examples.explanation: Loading data directly inside the labeled memory concept.

examples.title: The Why
examples.explanation: Variables make logic readable. 'maxSpeed = 50' is better than raw disconnected '50's.

foundation.keyPoint: Variables reserve raw bytes in RAM to track physical changes safely.

foundation.title: NAME_RULES
foundation.subtitle: Naming Rules
foundation.content: Java variables have strictly unforgiving naming rules utilizing camelCase for readability. They CANNOT aggressively start with a valid number, or use any weird spaces.
foundation.scenario: A Bank Vault Password. You can't use wild spaces or random illegal separators. The computer's rigid parser literally demands an unbroken unified character sequence.
foundation.learningOutcome: Write valid and readable variable names

examples.title: Meaningful Naming
examples.explanation: The Java best practice: camelCase using valid characters (a-z, _, 0-9).

examples.title: Invalid Start
examples.explanation: Starting maliciously with a number instantly ruins parsing.

examples.title: No Spaces
examples.explanation: Spaces physically snap the core variable string entirely.

foundation.keyPoint: Use meaningful camelCase. NO spaces. Cannot start with a pure digit.

foundation.title: DATATYPES
foundation.subtitle: Data Types
foundation.content: Java is Strongly Typed. You MUST identify the structural data type. Java separates basic primitives (Integer, Float, Boolean logicals) from Objects (massive String texts).
foundation.scenario: A Specialty Shipping Container. A 'Milk' container cannot hold 'Gasoline' without failure. You physically must label the data type perfectly so Java allocates RAM.
foundation.learningOutcome: Identify and use correct data types

examples.title: Integer (int)
examples.explanation: A massive signed positive/negative whole number without decimal logic.

examples.title: Float & Double
examples.explanation: Precise decimal numbers. 'double' is the standard massive Java default.

examples.title: String & Boolean
examples.explanation: Capital-S String for quoted text sequences, and lowercase boolean for True/False logic.

foundation.keyPoint: int, double, boolean, and Capital String are the absolute core.

foundation.title: ASSIGN
foundation.subtitle: Assigning Values
foundation.content: Java assignment uses the '=' operator exactly. It takes the finished calculation or boolean truth from the Right side and stores it physically into the distinct Left side container.
foundation.scenario: A Scoreboard. You don't dynamically guess the score; the referee securely calculates the points (Right Side) and ONLY THEN flips the scoreboard slots (Left Side).

examples.title: Assign Text
examples.explanation: The most fundamental placement of textual data into a mapped memory box.

examples.title: Assign Number
examples.explanation: Java solves mathematical numbers dynamically first, storing the answer totally.

examples.title: Assign Boolean
examples.explanation: Assigning a pure logical truth flag flawlessly.

foundation.keyPoint: The '=' operator evaluates all Right elements completely, then locks Left.

foundation.title: REASSIGN
foundation.subtitle: Reassigning Variables
foundation.content: To simulate dynamic behavior, variables literally change. You can update and deliberately overwrite the old original value with a new latest version. Java's absolute Type cannot swap, however.
foundation.scenario: A Lockbox. You extract the old rusted coin, chuck it, and insert a new polished coin. The box name is exactly identical, but the previous payload completely vanished.

examples.title: The Overwrite
examples.explanation: Overwriting a variable's payload. The latest value (100) destroys the old (10).

examples.title: Update Step-By-Step
examples.explanation: Changing values consecutively downwards in sequence.

examples.title: Self-Update Tracker
examples.explanation: Utilizing the old original value heavily to calculate the brand new latest value rapidly.

foundation.keyPoint: Variables overwrite values constantly, utilizing the very latest operation.

foundation.title: INPUT_CONCEPT
foundation.subtitle: Interaction flow
foundation.content: Input universally pauses the program conceptually. It creates intense interaction by taking user input directly, storing it safely into RAM, and eventually forcing output differences.
foundation.scenario: A Toll Booth interaction. The machine (program) STOPS processing entirely. It conceptually forces you to input money before the transaction flow stores the fee and outputs a green light.

examples.title: Conceptual Intake
examples.explanation: Taking user inputs directly natively via System.in Scanner mechanisms.

examples.title: Input -> Store
examples.explanation: Taking keyboard text flow fundamentally and safely storing it.

examples.title: Versus Output
examples.explanation: Input logically halts and sucks data inward. Output violently vomits data outward.

foundation.keyPoint: Input brings data IN interactively. Output aggressively pushes data OUT.

foundation.title: COMMENTS
foundation.subtitle: Ignored Text
foundation.content: Comments are single-line readable English notes specifically for programmers. The entire rigid Java Compiler brutally ignores them conceptually.
foundation.scenario: A Sticky Note on an Engine Valve. The note vividly screams 'Do not touch the red wire!'. The core engine entirely ignores the paper note, but the human perfectly observes it.

examples.title: Single Line
examples.explanation: Using strictly two slashes triggers Java to entirely ignore the remainder.

examples.title: Readable Inline
examples.explanation: Placing a clear readable note seamlessly at the ultimate end of an execution.

examples.title: Ignored Logic
examples.explanation: Commenting perfectly functional code effectively neutralizes it from executing.

foundation.keyPoint: // makes single lines completely flawlessly readable but inert.

foundation.title: KEYWORDS
foundation.subtitle: Keywords
foundation.content: Keywords are conceptually reserved protected words that Java utilizes for pure internal architecture (like 'print', 'if', 'class'). You absolutely cannot ever hijack them as variable names.
foundation.scenario: Reserved VIP Parking. The critical spot legally marked 'CEO' (Keyword syntax) cannot be casually seized by 'Employee Bob' (Variable). The complex system will heavily aggressively reject you.

examples.title: Reserved Concept
examples.explanation: Absolute reserved words strictly managing the massive program flow heavily.

examples.title: Data Modifiers
examples.explanation: Native protected keywords assigning strict memory types flawlessly.

examples.title: The Syntax Error
examples.explanation: Attempting to assign a variable legally named 'class' shatters compilation completely.

foundation.keyPoint: You cannot hijack Java's internal core system keywords.

foundation.title: CASE
foundation.subtitle: Case Sensitivity
foundation.content: Java exhibits immense case sensitivity. Uppercase mismatch triggers intense errors instantly. A single swapped letter literally creates completely disconnected names in the massive dictionary.
foundation.scenario: Two Cloned Twins. They appear physically visually identical, but one is tagged 'John' and the other specifically 'john'. In Java's relentless eyes, they are legally two strictly disconnected objects in RAM.
foundation.learningOutcome: Avoid logical errors due to case mismatch

examples.title: Name Differences
examples.explanation: Spawning two absolutely separate robust memory entities by tweaking a solitary case.

examples.title: Sensitivity Rules
examples.explanation: Java's immense core classes like String heavily mandate capitalization.

examples.title: Logical Mismatch Error
examples.explanation: Forgetting the upper/lower execution effectively invokes major compiler panic.

foundation.keyPoint: Case determines strict memory maps flawlessly. 'X' is heavily different than 'x'.

foundation.title: PREDICT
foundation.subtitle: Output Prediction
foundation.content: Predicting output strictly means carefully reading the code line-by-line organically in your mind. You actively track execution step-by-step, trace rapid variable changes natively, and avoid beginner logic mistakes.
foundation.scenario: Predictive 4D Chess mapped directly in your Organic Head. You picture entirely structurally exactly what the dynamic variables natively look like directly 3 moves vertically from now.

examples.title: Tracing Manual Execution
examples.explanation: Reading strictly downward to track absolute reassignment states sequentially.

examples.title: Variable Overwrite State
examples.explanation: Understanding complex math logic sequentially dynamically line by line.

examples.title: Mistake Avoidance
examples.explanation: Thinking forcefully simulating the entire CPU matrix before ever hitting run.
examples.code: // Analyze carefully, track mental state exactly.

foundation.keyPoint: Roleplay exactly sequentially to predict structural flow precisely.
